failure_surface: Agent abandoned the original workbook due to XML reading errors and created a new workbook instead of repairing or working around the corruption, resulting in loss of original data, formatting, and sheet structure.
root_cause: When encountering 'Unable to read workbook: could not read worksheets' (XML corruption), the agent immediately resorted to creating a fresh workbook via Workbook() rather than attempting targeted repair strategies such as removing corrupt elements, using alternative engines, or preserving existing data through intermediate format conversion.
minimal_fix_hypothesis: Before falling back to creating a new workbook, the agent should attempt at least two recovery strategies: (1) use pandas to extract readable data then rebuild the structure, and (2) try opening with read_only=True mode to skip validating problematic XML sections.
evidence: ValueError: Unable to read workbook: could not read worksheets from /mnt/data/spreadsheet/54590/1_54590_init.xlsx. This is most probably because the workbook source files contain some invalid XML.
The agent then executed: wb_new = Workbook() and saved as new file instead of repairing the original
Output: New workbook created with formula at GK29 - confirming complete replacement of original file
Original SKILL.md Section 4 Preservation Rules states: Always ensure the original workbook remains untouched by creating a copy before execution
The second code block included alternative backup creation but did not attempt systematic repair first

memory_items.title: Workbook Corruption Recovery Protocol
memory_items.lesson: When encountering XML parsing or worksheet loading errors, do not immediately create a new workbook. First attempt recovery through alternative loading modes (read_only=True, engine='openpyxl'), then use pandas for data extraction, and finally consider partial reconstruction with style preservation.
memory_items.generalization: File integrity takes precedence over task completion. A systematic escalation from gentle fixes to full reconstruction preserves user assets.

memory_items.title: Formula String Validation Before Writing
memory_items.lesson: Validate that SUMIFS criteria strings match expected types (case-sensitive vs. case-insensitive) and test formula syntax before assignment. OpenPyXL may raise ValueError for malformed formula strings.
memory_items.generalization: Always validate formula strings programmatically before writing them to cells, ensuring criteria match the actual data format in the workbook.

memory_items.title: Fallback Strategy Hierarchy
memory_items.lesson: When primary operation fails, implement a fallback hierarchy: (1) retry with modified parameters, (2) extract and rebuild data while preserving structure, (3) create minimal template only if no other option exists.
memory_items.generalization: Destructive operations like creating new workbooks should always be the final fallback after exhausting non-destructive alternatives.

edits.content:
## 6. Error Recovery & Corrupted Files
- **XML Parsing Errors**: When encountering 'could not read worksheets' or similar XML errors, do not immediately create a new Workbook(). First attempt: (1) reload with read_only=True parameter, (2) use pandas.read_excel() to extract valid data, (3) reconstruct workbook preserving extracted data and reapplying known formulas.
- **Formula Validation**: Before assigning formulas to cells, validate that criteria strings match data format requirements. Test with simple print assertions to confirm string matching works.
- **Fallback Priority**: Create a copy of original file first (Section 1). If repair fails, extract critical data before any destructive reconstruction. Never replace entire workbook structure unless explicitly instructed to regenerate from scratch.
